en.extract:
Niceforo's wren (Thryophilus nicefori) is a species of bird in the family Troglodytidae. It is endemic to Colombia.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical high-altitude dry shrubland. It is threatened by habitat loss.
In Spanish the bird is known as Cucarachero de Nicéforo or Cucarachero del Chicamocha.


Taxonomy
Niceforo's wren was first formally described in 1946 by the American ornithologist Rodolphe Meyer de Schauensee. The genus name derives from the Ancient Greek thruon - reed, and philos - loving. The species name commemorates Brother Nicéforo María, a Colombian missionary and herpetologist who provided Meyer de Schauensee with many specimens.
The type-locality site is San Gil on the río Fonce south of Bucaramanga, in Santander, where ten specimens were collected between 1944 and 1948.
Previously considered part of the genus Thryothorus and frequently treated as a subspecies of Rufous-and-white wren (Thryophilus rufalbus), Niceforo's wren is currently regarded as a full species. It is monotypic - there are no recognized subspecies.


Description
A medium-sized wren, averaging 14.5–15 cm (5.7–5.9 in) in length. Adult birds are dull olive-grey on the head and mantle, becoming slightly more rufescent on the back. The wings are finely barred, with more broad barring on the tail. A long white supercilium is bordered behind the eye by fine black lines. The face is marked by black and white streaks. The underparts are white becoming greyish on the sides and flanks. The undertail coverts show broad black barring. Juvenile birds have not been described.


Distribution and habitat


Distribution
Niceforo's wren is endemic to the valley of the Chicamocha River, as well as the Sogamoso, Suaréz, and Fonce river basins. These locations are within the departments of Boyacá and Santander, in the central northern part of Colombia.
After the first specimens of Niceforo's wren were collected during the 1940s, no further sightings were recorded until two birds were seen in that area in 1989 and 2000. This may reflect the fact that this area was difficult to visit from the 1950s onward due to guerilla activity.
In 2005 Oswaldo Cortes and colleagues discovered a disjunct population in the area of Soatá, approximately 100 km (62 mi) south of San Gill, and in 2004-06 Thomas M Donegan and colleagues discovered a population in the Serranía de los Yariguíes, about 50 km (31 mi) to the west of San Gill.


Habitat
This wren is found in dry forest at altitudes between 1,132 and 1,840 m (3,710 and 6,040 ft), in areas with abundant vegetation cover, a dense understory and abundant leaf litter. Its presence was found to be significantly correlated with that of Tricanthera gigantea, Acacia farnesiana, Sapindus saponaria and Pithecellobium dulce. A recent survey found populations of Niceforo's wren in riparian forest fragments with tangled vegetation composed by shrubs and thorn scrubs, and also along shaded edges of coffee and cacao plantations.


Behaviour


Feeding
Niceforo's wren is a largely terrestrial species, being found mostly within 2.66 m (8 ft 9 in) of ground level. They appear to forage for food mainly in leaf litter on the ground and have also been observed gleaning for arthropods under dry leaves and pecking on branches and tree holes.


Breeding
It is a monogamous species. Pairs defend territories from 1 to 4 hectares (2.5 to 9.9 acres) along streams and rivers, and the maximum density has been calculated as one individual for every two hectares of habitat.


Nesting
Niceforo's wrens construct an elbow-shaped nest with an entrance at one side of the top and a globular base. These nests are located between 3 and 10 m (9.8 and 32.8 ft) above the ground, typically in trees of 12–16 m (39–52 ft) in height. Nesting materials included vegetable fibers of Tillandsia usneoides, fungal rhizomorphs (Marasmius spp.), plant twigs, and vines, forming a very dense structure. As has been observed in two other species of Thryophilus wrens as well as some other tropical bird species, Niceforo's wrens construct their nests near wasp colonies, a strategy that increases the rate of offspring survival as the wasps deter predators.


Status
Niceforo's wren is listed as critically endangered on both the IUCN Red List and the Red Book of the Birds of Colombia. Surveys between 2004 and 2008 recorded 77 individuals. Based on these data the IUCN estimates the current global population is approximately 30-200 mature individuals. The surviving population is highly fragmented, with no single location holding more than 50 individuals.
The major threat to this species is habitat loss. All known populations face a progressive loss of habitat caused by the transformation of forests into croplands, pressure from goat farming (which destroys the dense understructure the species prefers), forest fires and the drying up of streams and rivers.


Conservation efforts
Project Chicamocha was established in 2003 under the Conservation Leadership Programme to support the conservation of two species endemic to the dry forest of the Chicamocha Valley: Niceforo's wren and the Chestnut-bellied hummingbird. This work led to the establishment in 2009 of the Niceforo's Wren Natural Bird Reserve in Zapatoca. Run by the Colombian NGO Fundación ProAves, the reserve comprises 1,400 ha (3,500 acres) of dry forest habitat, protecting a population of 21 individuals discovered in Santander Department in 2008. Another population of the wren is protected within the Serranía de los Yariguíes National Nature Park.
The project also led to the consolidation of the Alliance Chicamocha for the conservation of biodiversity of the Chicamocha Canyon, made up of government entities, NGOs, the local community, research institutes and universities.
In the municipality of Soatá in Boyacá, the organization OCOTEA, the ornithology group of the Francisco José de Caldas District University, and the Fundación Ecodiversidad have worked towards the conservation of and research on Niceforo's wren. In this town there is strong support for conservation of biodiversity, including the founding of the first community reserve in the Chicamocha Canyon.


References


External links
Project Chicamocha: Threatened Avifauna of the Chicamocha's Dry Forest Valley, Colombia
Project Chicamocha II: Saving threatened dry forest biodiversity

'''Niceforo's wren''' ('''''Thryophilus nicefori''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Troglodytidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to Colombia.<ref name="iucn" />

Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical high-altitude dry [[shrubland]]. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

In [[Spanish language|Spanish]] the bird is known as Cucarachero de Nicéforo or Cucarachero del Chicamocha.<ref name=Libro>{{cite book |last1=Renjifro |first1=Luis Miguel |last2=Amaya-Villareal |first2=Ángela María |last3=Burbano-Girón |first3=Jaime |last4=Velásquez-Tibatá |first4=Jorge |title=Libro rojo de aves de Colombia, Vol 2 |date=2016 |publisher=Editorial Pontificia Universidad Javeriana: Instituto Humboldt |isbn=978-958-716-980-5 |page=358}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
Niceforo's wren was first formally described in 1946 by the American ornithologist [[Rodolphe Meyer de Schauensee]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Meyer de Schauensee |first1=Rodolphe |title=A new species of Wren from Colombia |journal=Notulae Naturae |date=1946 |issue=182 |pages=1–2}}</ref> The genus name derives from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''thruon'' - reed, and ''philos'' - loving. The species name commemorates Brother Nicéforo María, a Colombian missionary and herpetologist who provided Meyer de Schauensee with many specimens.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Jobling |first1=James A. |title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |pages=270, 385}}</ref>

The type-locality site is [[San Gil]] on the río Fonce south of [[Bucaramanga]], in [[Santander Department|Santander]], where ten specimens were collected between 1944 and 1948.<ref name="iucn" />

Previously considered part of the genus ''[[Thryothorus]]'' and frequently treated as a subspecies of [[Rufous-and-white wren]] (''Thryophilus rufalbus''), Niceforo's wren is currently regarded as a full species. It is [[monotypic]] - there are no recognized subspecies.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gill |first1=F |last2=Donsker |first2=D |last3=Rasmussen |first3=P (Eds.) |title=IOC WORLD BIRD LIST (10.2) |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |website=IOC World Bird List |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Valderrama |first1=Sandra V. |last2=Parra |first2=Jorge E. |last3=Mennill |first3=Daniel J. |title=Species Differences in the Songs of the Critically Endangered Niceforo's Wren and the Related Rufous-And-White Wren |journal=The Condor |date=2007 |volume=109 |issue=4 |pages=870–877|doi=10.1093/condor/109.4.870 |doi-access=free |url=https://scholar.uwindsor.ca/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2130&context=biologypub }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mann |first1=Nigel I. |last2=Barker |first2=F. Keith |last3=Graves |first3=Jeff A. |last4=Dingess-Mann |first4=Kimberley A. |last5=Slater |first5=Peter J.B. |title=Molecular data delineate four genera of "Thryothorus" wrens |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |date=2006 |volume=40 |issue=3 |pages=750–759|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2006.04.014 |pmid=16750640 |bibcode=2006MolPE..40..750M }}</ref>

==Description==
A medium-sized wren, averaging {{cvt|14.5-15|cm}} in length. Adult birds are dull olive-grey on the head and mantle, becoming slightly more rufescent on the back. The wings are finely barred, with more broad barring on the tail. A long white [[supercilium]] is bordered behind the eye by fine black lines. The face is marked by black and white streaks. The underparts are white becoming greyish on the sides and flanks. The undertail coverts show broad black barring. Juvenile birds have not been described.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Restall |first1=Robin |last2=Rodner |first2=Clemencia |last3=Lentino |first3=Miguel |title=Birds of Northern South America: an Identification Guide |date=2007 |publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven |isbn=978-0-300-10862-0 |volume= 1 |page=597}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
===Distribution===
Niceforo's wren is endemic to the valley of the [[Chicamocha River]], as well as the Sogamoso, Suaréz, and Fonce river basins.<ref name=Parra>{{cite web |last1=Parra |first1=Jorge |last2=Dávila |first2=Nicolás |last3=Beltrán |first3=Marcela |last4=Delgadillo |first4=Alexandra |last5=Valderrama |first5=Sandra |last6=Cortes |first6=Oswaldo |title=PROJECT CHICAMOCHA The conservation of two Critically Endangered dry forest birds; Niceforo's Wren and Chestnut-bellied Hummingbird |url=https://www.conservationleadershipprogramme.org/media/2014/11/070104_Threatened_Avifauna_-Chicamocha_FinalReport.pdf |website=Conservation Leadership Programme |access-date=18 January 2021 |archive-date=27 January 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210127191920/https://www.conservationleadershipprogramme.org/media/2014/11/070104_Threatened_Avifauna_-Chicamocha_FinalReport.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> These locations are within the departments of Boyacá and Santander, in the central northern part of [[Colombia]].

After the first specimens of Niceforo's wren were collected during the 1940s, no further sightings were recorded until two birds were seen in that area in 1989 and 2000. This may reflect the fact that this area was difficult to visit from the 1950s onward due to guerilla activity.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Balchin |first1=Chris |title=Back from the dead! A potpourri of recent rediscoveries in the Neotropics |journal=Neotropical Birding |date=2007 |issue=2 |pages=10–11 |url=https://www.neotropicalbirdclub.org/nbc-publications/neotropical-birding/neotropical-birding-2/ |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref>

In 2005 Oswaldo Cortes and colleagues discovered a disjunct population in the area of [[Soatá]], approximately {{cvt|100|km}} south of San Gill, and in 2004-06 Thomas M Donegan and colleagues discovered a population in the [[Serranía de los Yariguíes]], about {{cvt|50|km}} to the west of San Gill.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cortes-Herrera |first1=Jose Oswaldo |last2=Hernández-Jaramillo |first2=Alejandro |last3=Cháves-Portilla |first3=Giovanni |last4=Laverde |first4=Oscar |last5=Gamba-Triviño |first5=Catherinae |last6=Villagran Chavarro |first6=Daira Ximena |last7=Alarcón-Bernal |first7=Sandra |title=Nuevos registros de poblaciones de aves amenazadas en Soatá (Boyacá), Colombia |journal=Cotinga |date=2007 |issue=27 |page=75 |url=https://www.neotropicalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/C27-Cortes-Herrera.pdf |access-date=18 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Donegan |first1=Thomas M. |last2=Avendaño-C |first2=Jorge Enrique |last3=Briceño-L |first3=Elkin R. |last4=Huertes |first4=Bianca |title=Range extensions, taxonomic and ecological notes from Serranía de los Yariguíes, Colombia's new national park. |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=2007 |volume=127 |pages=199–200 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40877269#page/6/mode/1up |access-date=20 January 2021}}</ref>

===Habitat===
This wren is found in dry forest at altitudes between {{cvt|1132|and|1840|m|-1}}, in areas with abundant vegetation cover, a dense understory and abundant leaf litter. Its presence was found to be significantly correlated with that of ''Tricanthera gigantea'', ''Acacia farnesiana'', ''Sapindus saponaria'' and ''Pithecellobium dulce''. A recent survey found populations of Niceforo's wren in [[riparian forest]] fragments with tangled vegetation composed by shrubs and thorn scrubs, and also along shaded edges of coffee and cacao plantations.<ref name="Parra" /><ref name="iucn" />

==Behaviour==
===Feeding===
Niceforo's wren is a largely terrestrial species, being found mostly within {{cvt|2.66|m}} of ground level. They appear to forage for food mainly in leaf litter on the ground and have also been observed gleaning for arthropods under dry leaves and pecking on branches and tree holes.<ref name="Parra" />

===Breeding===
It is a monogamous species. Pairs defend territories from {{convert|1|to|4|ha}} along streams and rivers, and the maximum density has been calculated as one individual for every two hectares of habitat.<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Valderrama-Ortiz |first1=Sandra V. |title=. Contribución al conocimiento de la historia natural, ecología y hábitat del cucarachero de Nicéforo en un enclave seco del municipio de San Gil (Santander). |url=https://repositorio.uniandes.edu.co/bitstream/handle/1992/22686/u270966.pdf?sequence=1 |degree=Undergraduate |publisher=University of the Andes|location= Bogotá |lang=es |access-date=20 January 2021}}</ref>

===Nesting===
Niceforo's wrens construct an elbow-shaped nest with an entrance at one side of the top and a globular base. These nests are located between {{cvt|3|and|10|m}} above the ground, typically in trees of {{cvt|12-16|m}} in height. Nesting materials included vegetable fibers of ''Tillandsia usneoides'', fungal [[rhizomorphs]] (''Marasmius'' spp.), plant twigs, and vines, forming a very dense structure. As has been observed in two other species of ''[[Thryophilus]]'' wrens as well as some other tropical bird species, Niceforo's wrens construct their nests near wasp colonies, a strategy that increases the rate of offspring survival as the wasps deter predators.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Valderrama |first1=Sandra V. |last2=Parra |first2=Jorge E. |last3=Dávila |first3=Nicolás |title=First Nest Description for Niceforo´s Wren (''Thryothorus nicefori''): A Critically Endangered Colombian Endemic Songbird |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical |date=2007 |issue=18}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Beier |first1=Paul |last2=Issahaku Tungbani |first2=Agba |title=Nesting with the Wasp ''Ropalidia cincta'' Increases Nest Success of Red-cheeked Cordonbleu (''Uraeginthus bengalus'') in Ghana |journal=The Auk |date=2006 |volume=123 |issue=4}}</ref>

==Status==
Niceforo's wren is listed as critically endangered on both the IUCN Red List and the Red Book of the Birds of Colombia. Surveys between 2004 and 2008 recorded 77 individuals. Based on these data the IUCN estimates the current global population is approximately 30-200 mature individuals. The surviving population is highly fragmented, with no single location holding more than 50 individuals.<ref name="iucn" /><ref name="Libro" />

The major threat to this species is [[habitat loss]]. All known populations face a progressive loss of habitat caused by the transformation of forests into croplands, pressure from goat farming (which destroys the dense understructure the species prefers), forest fires and the drying up of streams and rivers.<ref name="Libro" />

===Conservation efforts===
Project Chicamocha was established in 2003 under the Conservation Leadership Programme to support the conservation of two species endemic to the dry forest of the Chicamocha Valley: Niceforo's wren and the [[Chestnut-bellied hummingbird]]. This work led to the establishment in 2009 of the Niceforo's Wren Natural Bird Reserve in [[Zapatoca]]. Run by the Colombian [[NGO]] [[Fundación ProAves]], the reserve comprises {{cvt|1400|ha}} of dry forest habitat, protecting a population of 21 individuals discovered in [[Santander Department]] in 2008. Another population of the wren is protected within the [[Serranía de los Yariguíes]] National Nature Park.<ref name="iucn" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Chicamocha Project |work=Fundación ProAves - por la conservación en el país de las aves |date=14 June 2005 |url=https://proaves.org/en/chicamocha-project/ |publisher=Fundacion ProAves |access-date=20 January 2021 |author1=Dogie_To }}</ref>

The project also led to the consolidation of the Alliance Chicamocha for the conservation of biodiversity of the [[Chicamocha Canyon]], made up of government entities, NGOs, the local community, research institutes and universities.<ref name="Libro" />

In the municipality of [[Soatá]] in [[Boyacá Department|Boyacá]], the organization OCOTEA, the ornithology group of the [[Francisco José de Caldas District University]], and the Fundación Ecodiversidad have worked towards the conservation of and research on Niceforo's wren. In this town there is strong support for conservation of biodiversity, including the founding of the first community reserve in the Chicamocha Canyon.<ref name="Libro" />
